title: Greenland Ice Melt: A Story from Long Ago and Today
summary: Scientists found that a big ice cap in Greenland called Prudhoe Dome melted completely about 7,000 years ago. This happened during a time called the Holocene, when the climate was stable and people started farming. The study used a project called GreenDrill to drill into the ice and collect rocks and soil from below. By checking these materials, they learned when the ice last melted. This is important because it shows that even small warming can make ice disappear. Today, human activities are warming the Earth, so this ice might melt again, which could raise sea levels and affect coastal areas. The research helps scientists predict future melting and protect communities.

keywords.explanation: a large area of ice covering land
a time period starting 11,000 years ago with stable climate
a project to drill through ice and study materials below
how high the ocean is, which can rise when ice melts
when the Earth gets hotter, often from human activities

background_read: Greenland is a large island covered in ice, and its ice sheet holds a lot of frozen water. When ice melts, it flows into the ocean and makes sea levels higher, which can flood coastal cities and harm wildlife. Scientists study past ice melt to understand how climate change affects our planet. Projects like GreenDrill help by digging deep into the ice to find clues from long ago. This research is crucial because it teaches us how to prepare for changes in the future and protect our environment.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from the GreenDrill project, led by Jason Briner and Joerg Schaefer, studied Greenland's ice.
WHAT: They discovered that the Prudhoe Dome ice cap melted completely 7,000 years ago during the Holocene period.
WHERE: This happened in northwest Greenland, at a place called Prudhoe Dome.
WHY: The study shows that even mild warming can cause ice to melt, and with today's human-induced climate change, it might happen again, raising sea levels.

perspectives.description: The study reveals new information about past ice melt to help predict future changes.
